title: Future Friendly Fund invests in European charging network for electric aircraft
intro: Amsterdam, 18 March 2026 – As rising energy prices and geopolitical tensions put pressure on the aviation sector, Dutch travellers are taking matters into their own hands by investing in new solutions.
body: The Future Friendly Fund foundation has recently invested in NRG2fly, an organisation building a European network of charging points for electric aircraft and electric ground equipment at airports. The real cost of flying The Future Friendly Fund was set up with a clear purpose: to make the true cost of flying visible. Beyond the ticket price, there are also societal costs, for example from climate change, air pollution and effects on nature. Through the fund, people can voluntarily pay these hidden costs. The money is then invested in projects that contribute to solutions for the aviation of the future. Donors can choose where their contribution goes. The choice is between biodiversity projects, such as Grounded and Lenteland, and aviation innovation. Most donors opt to direct their contribution towards technological renewal within the sector. First results since launch The fund's initiators themselves contributed €150,000 as seed capital. Since the launch in January, travellers have additionally donated €6,000 by consciously paying the real price of their flight. This includes not only holiday flights but also business travel. A growing number of companies are choosing to incorporate the actual impact of their flights into their cost calculations. Many people also use the fund's online calculator to work out the climate impact of their flight and what the real cost of flying means in practice. European network With its investment in NRG2fly, the fund is addressing a critical prerequisite for electric aviation: energy in the right place. NRG2fly is not only developing solutions for individual airports but is building a European network of charging points. Starting from the initial locations at Teuge and Lelystad, this infrastructure is being expanded step by step to airports in Germany and other parts of Europe. The goal is to give electric aircraft access to a connected network of charging facilities, wherever they land. www.futurefriendlyfund.com www.nrg2fly.com

title: NRG2fly Delivers First MCS Aircraft Charger to Væridion's New Production Hangar
intro: A megawatt-class demonstrator charger capable of a full charge in under 30 minutes marks a tangible step toward NRG2fly's European charging network for electric aviation.
body:
The opening of Væridion's first production hangar and test house at Oberpfaffenhofen (EDMO) near Munich was a milestone for electric aviation in Europe. The facility will serve as the home base for developing their fully electric nine-seater, with a stated goal of getting airborne within 100 weeks. For NRG2fly, the occasion carried extra significance: it marked the delivery of our first MCS Demonstrator Aircraft Charger to the site.

Fast turnaround is not a luxury in commercial aviation. It is the difference between an economically viable route and one that sits idle on the tarmac. For electric aircraft operators, the charging window is the new refuelling window, and shrinking it to 30 minutes opens up scheduling possibilities that simply do not exist with slower charging infrastructure.

This is still a demonstrator. There is plenty of engineering, certification work, and real-world testing ahead before this technology operates at scale across European airports. But that is precisely the point: every large network starts with a first node. Delivering this charger to Væridion's hangar puts a physical, working piece of NRG2fly's European Charging Network on the map.

Congratulations to the entire Væridion team on this milestone. Building the first production hangar for a clean-sheet electric aircraft programme is no small feat. We are proud to be part of the journey alongside them, and we look forward to what the next 100 weeks bring.

title: Maxem & NRG2fly partner to accelerate full airport electrification
intro: Maxem and NRG2fly have announced a strategic collaboration to support the electrification of airports.  Airports are increasingly electrifying ground support equipment (GSE), vehicles, buildings, and energy systems.
body: Preparing for electric aircraft is a natural next step to ensure airports remain future-ready as aviation continues to electrify. This growing electricity demand requires a smart, integrated approach. NRG2fly brings hands-on experience with charging infrastructure at airports, while Maxem provides intelligent energy management to optimize grid capacity, manage peak loads, and integrate local renewable energy. Together, the partners help airports transition into future-proof, fully electrified energy hubs, reducing dependency on costly grid upgrades today and creating a scalable foundation for electric flight tomorrow.Merlijn van Vliet, of NRG2fly, said: “Electrifying an airport goes far beyond aircraft alone. By combining smart energy management with airport-wide charging infrastructure, we enable airports to electrify step by step and be ready for electric aviation when it scales.” NRG2fly is building a comprehensive European charging network for electric aircraft and electric ground support equipment (GSE). Through this partnership, Maxem strengthens this network by enabling smart energy management at airport level, allowing charging infrastructure to scale within existing grid constraints.
